exemplars.letter_content:
Dr Helena Park
Consultant in Sleep Medicine
City General Hospital

26 May 2026

Dear Dr Park,

Re: Mr Thomas Holm, aged 55

I am writing to refer Mr Holm, a 55-year-old sales manager, who has been provisionally diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnoea. Your assessment and management would be appreciated.

Mr Holm has a six-month history of excessive daytime sleepiness with witnessed apnoeic episodes and habitual loud snoring reported by his wife. Subsequently, he has experienced two near-miss incidents while driving. Please note, he denied chest pain or palpitations.

Today, Mr Holm attended for review with marked symptom progression. His Epworth Sleepiness Scale score was 18 and his STOP-BANG score was 6 of 8, indicating high pre-test probability of obstructive sleep apnoea. Clinical examination revealed a body mass index of 32, a neck circumference of 44 cm and a Mallampati class III oropharynx.

As you are aware, Mr Holm's records show he has hypertension, on amlodipine 10 mg daily, and type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 500 mg twice daily. He has been advised to notify the DVLA in view of the driving risk.

I would be grateful if you could arrange overnight polysomnography and consider commencement of continuous positive airway pressure therapy if indicated.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

Dr Sarah Ahmed

annotations.note: Re: line uses 'aged 55' lowercase with no colon after 'aged' per canon.
Opening follows routine referral pattern: 'I am writing to refer [Name], a [age]-year-old [occupation], who [provisional diagnosis].'
'Subsequently,' is one of the four approved linkers.
Denials use the canonical 'Please note, he denied...' pattern.
Paragraph opens with 'Today,' — required canonical paragraph opener.
Examination findings introduced with 'Clinical examination revealed...' canonical opener.
'As you are aware,' used correctly to introduce pre-existing medications already on the GP's records.
Past history uses 'records show he has...' pattern with drug and dose.
Closure uses 'I would be grateful if you could...' canonical referral request.
Final pre-signoff sentence uses 'do not hesitate to contact me'.
Sign-off line is exactly 'Yours sincerely,' (capital Y, lowercase s).
Patient is named 'Mr Holm' on first mention in each paragraph; 'the patient' never used.
Name appears once per paragraph; pronouns 'he' used thereafter within each paragraph.

exemplars.letter_content:
Dr James Whitfield
Consultant Cardiologist
Royal Northern Hospital

26 May 2026

Dear Dr Whitfield,

Re: Mrs Eleanor Carter, aged 62

I am writing to refer Mrs Carter, a 62-year-old retired teacher, who has experienced increasing breathlessness on exertion. Your specialist opinion would be appreciated.

Mrs Carter first presented six weeks ago with dyspnoea on climbing a single flight of stairs. Consequently, she was investigated in primary care with a chest X-ray, an electrocardiogram and a B-type natriuretic peptide. Please note, she denied chest pain, orthopnoea or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea at that time.

Today, Mrs Carter returned for review with worsening symptoms over the past fortnight. She now reports breathlessness on minimal exertion and intermittent ankle swelling by the evening. Clinical examination revealed a regular pulse of 88 beats per minute, blood pressure of 138/86 mmHg, mild bilateral ankle oedema and reduced air entry at the right lung base.

As you are aware, Mrs Carter's records show she has type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 500 mg twice daily, and hypercholesterolaemia, on atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. Her B-type natriuretic peptide returned at 410 ng/L, which I considered borderline.

I would be grateful if you could arrange transthoracic echocardiography and consider initiation of heart failure therapy if indicated.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

Dr Sarah Ahmed

annotations.note: Re: line correctly formatted with 'aged 62'.
Opening explicitly identifies purpose, age and occupation per the routine referral pattern.
'Consequently,' chosen from the four approved linkers.
Negatives reported via 'Please note, she denied...' canonical denial pattern.
Today's encounter introduced with 'Today,' paragraph opener.
Examination block uses 'Clinical examination revealed...' canonical opener.
'As you are aware,' frames pre-existing medications, not new findings.
Past history uses 'records show she has...' with drug and dose for each condition.
Closure invites action with 'I would be grateful if you could...' canonical request.
Closing sentence invites contact with 'do not hesitate to contact me'.
Sign-off uses 'Yours sincerely,' verbatim.
Opening focuses on purpose and presentation; no medication enumeration in opening paragraph.

exemplars.letter_content:
Dr Helena Park
General Practitioner
Riverside Medical Centre

26 May 2026

Dear Dr Park,

Re: Mrs Bernadette Ahmed, aged 70

I am writing to inform you of structured warfarin counselling provided to Mrs Ahmed today, and to request your review of her current analgesia. Your input would be appreciated.

Mrs Ahmed attended the pharmacy to collect her first warfarin prescription following her recent diagnosis of atrial fibrillation. Subsequently, thirty minutes of structured counselling was delivered covering dosing, dietary vitamin K consistency, INR monitoring intervals, bleeding-sign recognition and over-the-counter medication risks. A yellow oral anticoagulant card and warfarin information booklet were issued.

Today, two areas of concern were identified. Mrs Ahmed reports regular self-purchase of ibuprofen 200 mg, taking three to four tablets daily for chronic right knee pain. In addition, she has mild visual impairment and requires a magnifier to read her dosing book; consideration of carer assistance with monitoring entries is suggested.

As you are aware, Mrs Ahmed's records show she has primary hypothyroidism, on levothyroxine 75 micrograms daily, and hypertension, on atenolol 50 mg daily. Please note, she denied any active bleeding or bruising on direct questioning.

It would be appreciated if you could review her analgesia plan and select a warfarin-compatible alternative.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact the pharmacy.

Yours sincerely,

Dr Sarah Ahmed (Pharmacist Independent Prescriber)

annotations.note: Re: line correctly formatted with 'aged 70'.
Pharmacy letter raises a concern AND states the request ('review of her current analgesia') in the opening paragraph.
Both 'Subsequently,' and 'In addition,' chosen from the four approved linkers.
Paragraph opens with 'Today,' to introduce today's encounter.
'As you are aware,' precedes pre-existing medications already known to the prescriber.
Denial uses canonical 'Please note, she denied...' form.
Closure uses 'It would be appreciated if you could...' (collaborative handover style for response/communication letters).
Final pre-signoff sentence directs reader to 'contact the pharmacy' rather than 'contact me' — pharmacy-specific closure per canon.
Sign-off line is exactly 'Yours sincerely,'.
Patient is named 'Mrs Ahmed' on first mention in each paragraph; 'the patient' is never used.

exemplars.letter_content:
Dr Akinwale Adeyemi
General Practitioner
Riverside Medical Centre

26 May 2026

Dear Dr Adeyemi,

Re: Mr Geoffrey Williams, aged 77

I am writing to alert you to a high-risk co-prescription of warfarin and naproxen identified at clinical check today, and to request review of Mr Williams' analgesia. Your prompt input would be appreciated.

Mr Williams presented today with his repeat prescription, which included warfarin 3 mg daily alongside newly added naproxen 500 mg twice daily. Consequently, the pharmacy team identified a clinically significant interaction with elevated bleeding risk and withheld the naproxen pending your review. Mr Williams has provided informed consent for this action.

Today, he reports two small episodes of gum bleeding on tooth-brushing in the past 48 hours. Please note, he denied haematemesis, melaena, haematuria or epigastric pain. Clinical examination revealed no overt bruising or pallor. His most recent INR on 18 May 2026 was 2.6, within the target range of 2 to 3; however, a repeat INR has been booked as a priority for tomorrow.

As you are aware, Mr Williams' records show he has atrial fibrillation, on warfarin since 2018, and right knee osteoarthritis. Paracetamol 1 g four times daily has been supplied today as interim cover.

It would be appreciated if you could discontinue the naproxen and select an analgesic alternative appropriate to anticoagulated patients.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact the pharmacy.

Yours sincerely,

Dr Sarah Ahmed (Pharmacist Independent Prescriber)

annotations.note: Re: line correctly formatted with 'aged 77'.
Concern AND explicit request stated together in the opening paragraph (pharmacy-specific rule).
'Consequently,' and '; however,' both from the four approved linkers.
Paragraph opens with 'Today,' to introduce today's encounter.
Negative findings reported as 'Please note, he denied...'.
Examination findings introduced with 'Clinical examination revealed...'.
'As you are aware,' frames pre-existing condition and treatment.
Closure 'It would be appreciated if you could...' suits a pharmacy-to-prescriber collaborative request.
Pharmacy closure directs the reader to 'contact the pharmacy' per canon.
Sign-off line is exactly 'Yours sincerely,'.

exemplars.letter_content:
Senior District Nurse
North Riverside Community Hub

26 May 2026

Dear Nurse,

Re: Mrs Sandra Bell, aged 68

I am writing to inform you that Mrs Bell was discharged home today following an uncomplicated right total knee replacement. Continuation of her venous thromboembolism prophylaxis and wound care in the community would be appreciated.

Mrs Bell underwent surgery on 23 May 2026 under spinal anaesthesia, with no intra-operative complications. Subsequently, she made steady post-operative progress and is now independently transferring with elbow crutches and weight-bearing as tolerated. Please note, she denied any wound pain at rest and her observations have remained stable throughout the admission.

Today, Mrs Bell was reviewed prior to discharge. Clinical examination revealed a clean, dry surgical wound with the original Opsite dressing intact and no surrounding erythema. Her knee flexion was 85 degrees with full extension.

She has been discharged on tinzaparin 4500 units subcutaneously daily for fourteen days from the surgery date; her husband is unable to administer the injection due to needle phobia. In addition, the surgical dressing is to remain in place until day ten from surgery and the wound should then be reviewed.

As you are aware, Mrs Bell has a documented allergy to penicillin, which causes a rash.

It would be appreciated if you could administer the daily subcutaneous tinzaparin until day fourteen, check the wound on day seven and remove the dressing on day ten.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

Nurse Sarah Ahmed (Orthopaedic Discharge Coordinator)

annotations.note: Re: line correctly formatted with 'aged 68'.
Discharge opening focuses on the discharge fact and continuation request — no medication enumeration.
'Subsequently,' and 'In addition,' both from the four approved linkers.
Negative finding reported via 'Please note, she denied...' canonical pattern.
Today's review introduced with 'Today,' paragraph opener.
Examination introduced with 'Clinical examination revealed...'.
'As you are aware,' frames the allergy (a fixed fact the community nurse needs to know).
Closure uses 'It would be appreciated if you could...' canonical discharge handover form.
Social history (husband) is included only where directly relevant (needle phobia explains why district nurse cover is required).
Sign-off line is exactly 'Yours sincerely,'.

exemplars.letter_content:
Dr Imran Khan
General Practitioner
The Meadows Surgery

26 May 2026

Dear Dr Khan,

Re: Mr Charles Nguyen, aged 74

I am writing to inform you that Mr Nguyen was discharged home today following a five-day admission for an acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Continuation of his oral antibiotic course and a follow-up review in the community would be appreciated.

Mr Nguyen presented to the emergency department on 22 May 2026 with increased breathlessness, productive cough and a sputum colour change. Subsequently, he was treated with oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily, prednisolone 30 mg daily for five days and nebulised bronchodilators. Please note, he denied chest pain, haemoptysis or fevers throughout the admission.

Today, Mr Nguyen was assessed prior to discharge. Clinical examination revealed a respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, oxygen saturations of 95 percent on room air and reduced wheeze on auscultation. He has tolerated his usual diet and is mobile independently at his baseline level.

As you are aware, Mr Nguyen's records show he has long-standing chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, on inhaled tiotropium 18 micrograms daily and salbutamol as required. In addition, he will complete the seven-day amoxicillin course at home and continue his usual maintenance inhalers.

It would be appreciated if you could review Mr Nguyen in two weeks to reinforce inhaler technique and consider pulmonary rehabilitation referral.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

Nurse Sarah Ahmed (Respiratory Discharge Coordinator)

'As you are aware,' precedes pre-existing condition and maintenance medications.
Past history uses 'records show he has...' with drug and dose.
No social history (smoking, occupation) included — appropriate for a GP discharge.
Closure uses 'It would be appreciated if you could...' canonical discharge handover form.
Sign-off line is exactly 'Yours sincerely,'.

exemplars.letter_content:
Dr Oluwa Ben-Ade
On-call Cardiology Registrar
Royal Northern Hospital A&E

26 May 2026

Dear Doctor,

Re: Mr Raj Patel, aged 47

I am writing to urgently refer Mr Patel, a 47-year-old taxi driver, who presents with a 90-minute history of central chest pain and electrocardiographic changes suggestive of an inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction. Immediate cardiology assessment is required.

Mr Patel attended walk-in surgery this afternoon with sudden-onset crushing central chest pain at rest, radiating to his left arm and jaw. He rates the pain as 8 out of 10. In addition, he reports nausea, diaphoresis and shortness of breath unresponsive to rest. Please note, he denied recent trauma, recent flights or lower limb swelling.

Today, clinical examination revealed a distressed, diaphoretic patient with peripheral pallor, a blood pressure of 148 over 92 mmHg in the right arm and 146 over 90 mmHg in the left, a regular pulse of 102 beats per minute, oxygen saturations of 95 percent on room air, and a temperature of 36.8 degrees Celsius. His twelve-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated 1 mm ST elevation in leads II, III and aVF with reciprocal depression in I and aVL.

As you are aware, Mr Patel's records show he has type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 500 mg twice daily, hypertension, on ramipril 5 mg daily, and hyperlipidaemia, on simvastatin 40 mg nightly. His father had a myocardial infarction at age 52.

Aspirin 300 mg has been administered orally, sublingual glyceryl trinitrate has been given with partial relief and intravenous access has been secured. An ambulance has been called.

I would be grateful if you could accept Mr Patel for immediate primary percutaneous coronary intervention assessment.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

Dr Sarah Ahmed

annotations.note: Re: line correctly formatted with 'aged 47'.
Urgent referral opener includes explicit urgency marker ('urgently') and immediate-action language.
'In addition,' from the four approved linkers.
Negatives via 'Please note, he denied...' canonical pattern.
Examination paragraph introduced with 'Today,' opener.
Examination findings introduced with 'Clinical examination revealed...'.
'As you are aware,' frames pre-existing medications already known.

Closure uses 'I would be grateful if you could...' canonical urgent referral request.
Closing courtesy 'do not hesitate to contact me'.
Sign-off line is exactly 'Yours sincerely,'.

exemplars.letter_content:
Dr Imran Khan
General Practitioner
The Meadows Surgery

26 May 2026

Dear Dr Khan,

Re: Mrs Eleanor Carter, aged 62

I am writing to inform you that Mrs Carter was discharged today following a seven-day admission for community-acquired pneumonia. Your ongoing care, including a planned chest X-ray and renal function monitoring, would be appreciated.

Mrs Carter presented on 19 May 2026 with a four-day history of productive cough with greenish sputum, pleuritic right-sided chest pain, fever, rigors and reduced oral intake. Subsequently, chest X-ray demonstrated right lower zone consolidation and blood cultures grew Streptococcus pneumoniae sensitive to amoxicillin.

Mrs Carter was treated with intravenous co-amoxiclav 1.2 g three times daily for forty-eight hours, followed by oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily to complete a seven-day course. In addition, she received supplemental oxygen for the first twenty-four hours and intravenous fluids on day one. She became apyrexial from day three; her oxygen requirement was weaned by day four, and her CRP fell from 198 to 22 by day seven.

Today, clinical examination revealed oxygen saturations of 97 percent on room air, normal cardiovascular and respiratory examination, and unrestricted mobilisation. Please note, she denied any chest pain, breathlessness or fevers at the time of discharge.

As you are aware, Mrs Carter's records show she has type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 1 g twice daily, hypertension, on ramipril 10 mg daily, and mild chronic kidney disease.

It would be appreciated if you could arrange a repeat chest X-ray in six weeks to confirm radiological resolution, and monitor her renal function in two weeks following the recent intravenous antibiotics.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

Dr Sarah Ahmed

'As you are aware,' frames pre-existing conditions and medications already known to the GP.

No social history (smoking, alcohol, hobbies) included — appropriate for GP discharge.
Closure uses 'It would be appreciated if you could...' canonical discharge handover form.
Closing courtesy 'do not hesitate to contact me'.
Sign-off line is exactly 'Yours sincerely,'.

exemplars.letter_content:
Dr Olivia Sandhu
Consultant Clinical Pharmacologist
City General Hospital

26 May 2026

Dear Dr Sandhu,

Re: Mr Stephen Boyle, aged 68

I am writing to refer Mr Boyle, a 68-year-old retired engineer, who has experienced persistent muscle aches despite serial atorvastatin dose reductions in primary care, in the context of high cardiovascular risk. Your specialist advice on lipid-lowering optimisation would be appreciated.

Mr Boyle had a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction in February 2025 and was initially established on atorvastatin 80 mg daily. Subsequently, he developed bilateral thigh discomfort and a mildly raised creatine kinase. The dose has been reduced to 40 mg and then to 20 mg, with partial symptomatic improvement.

Today, clinical assessment in the pharmacist-led clinic revealed a blood pressure of 128 over 76 mmHg, a regular pulse of 64 beats per minute, no proximal muscle weakness and a creatine kinase of 298 IU/L. His current LDL cholesterol is 2.6 mmol/L, above the post-MI target of less than 1.8.

As you are aware, Mr Boyle's records show he has type 2 diabetes mellitus, on metformin 1 g twice daily, and hypertension, on ramipril 10 mg daily. Please note, he denied dark urine, severe pain or new neurological symptoms.

Ezetimibe 10 mg daily has been added today, and ongoing weekly pharmacy telephone reviews have been arranged.

I would be grateful if you could review Mr Boyle and consider PCSK9 inhibitor therapy per NICE TA694 if the LDL target remains unachievable.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact the pharmacy.

Yours sincerely,

Dr Sarah Ahmed (Pharmacist Independent Prescriber)

exemplars.letter_content:
Dr Sarah Ahmed
General Practitioner
Riverside Medical Centre

26 May 2026

Dear Dr Ahmed,

Re: Mr Daniel Okonkwo, aged 61

Thank you for your letter of 19 May 2026 regarding Mr Okonkwo. I am writing in response to your enquiry about commencement of basal insulin titration and structured counselling. He attended the pharmacist-led diabetes clinic today and the following plan has been agreed.

Mr Okonkwo's home glucose diary over the past four weeks demonstrated fasting glucose of 10 to 13 mmol/L and pre-evening meal readings of 9 to 11 mmol/L. Please note, he denied nocturnal hypoglycaemia. Renal function remains acceptable at eGFR 68; liver function tests are normal.

Today, insulin glargine has been initiated at 12 units subcutaneously at bedtime. Injection technique has been demonstrated using a pre-filled pen device. In addition, a structured 'safe insulin start' counselling pack has been provided, covering sick-day rules and hypoglycaemia recognition.

As you are aware, Mr Okonkwo's records show he has type 2 diabetes mellitus since 2015, on metformin 1 g twice daily and dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, and background diabetic retinopathy. He will continue both oral antihyperglycaemics unchanged.

The agreed titration plan is to increase by 2 units every three days if fasting glucose remains above 7 mmol/L, with a target of 5 to 7 mmol/L. Weekly telephone reviews have been booked for the next six weeks before handover.

It would be appreciated if you could repeat his HbA1c in twelve weeks and arrange annual nephropathy screening.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact the pharmacy.

Yours sincerely,

Dr Sarah Ahmed (Pharmacist Independent Prescriber)

exemplars.letter_content:
Ms Janet Chowdhury
Specialist Continence Nurse
City Community Continence Service

26 May 2026

Dear Ms Chowdhury,

Re: Mr Henry Wilson, aged 77

I am writing to refer Mr Wilson, a 77-year-old retired postman, who has experienced persistent urinary incontinence eight weeks after a right middle cerebral artery ischaemic stroke. Your specialist assessment and management would be appreciated.

Mr Wilson has three to four daytime and one to two nocturnal incontinence episodes per day, with a mixed urge and functional pattern related to his residual left-sided weakness. Please note, he denied dysuria, haematuria or fever.

Today, district nurse bladder assessment revealed a negative urinalysis and a post-void residual scan of 60 mL. Clinical examination revealed reduced functional bladder capacity of 200 to 250 mL on the bladder diary, with voiding every one to two hours during the day. In addition, two sacral pressure-area redness episodes have occurred in the past month, related to incontinence.

As you are aware, Mr Wilson's records show he has hypertension, on amlodipine 10 mg daily and ramipril 5 mg daily, and is on clopidogrel 75 mg daily following his stroke. Antimuscarinic medications carry cognitive risk in his context.

Continence pads have been accepted today after initial reluctance, and barrier cream has been supplied. His wife is the primary carer and reports significant fatigue from frequent toileting assistance.

I would be grateful if you could arrange a specialist continence assessment within four to six weeks, consider a bladder retraining programme appropriate to post-stroke cognition, and provide carer support information for his wife.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

Nurse Sarah Ahmed (District Nursing Sister)

exemplars.letter_content:
Senior Community Nurse
North Riverside District Nursing Hub

26 May 2026

Dear Nurse,

Re: Mr Henry Adetola, aged 78

I am writing to transfer the care of Mr Adetola to your service following stabilisation of his left venous leg ulcer in the acute tissue viability unit. Continuation of his current twice-weekly compression dressing regime in the community would be appreciated.

Mr Adetola was admitted on 12 May 2026 with cellulitis around an eleven-month-old left medial gaiter venous leg ulcer. Subsequently, he was treated with a seven-day course of oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily, completed on 19 May 2026. Doppler ABPI and duplex scan confirmed suitability for compression therapy and a venous aetiology.

Today, clinical examination revealed a wound measuring 4 by 3 cm with 80 percent granulation tissue and 20 percent slough, moderate serous exudate and macerated surrounding skin with barrier cream applied. Please note, he denied fever, increasing pain or purulent discharge. The wound has reduced in size from 6 by 4 cm over the past fourteen days.

As you are aware, Mr Adetola's records show he has chronic venous insufficiency, type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin 1 g twice daily, hypertension on ramipril 5 mg daily, and atrial fibrillation on apixaban 5 mg twice daily. He lives alone in a ground-floor flat with daily support from his daughter.

It would be appreciated if you could continue twice-weekly 4-layer Profore compression dressings, monitor for signs of infection, photograph the wound weekly, reassess ABPI every three months and re-refer to tissue viability if the wound fails to heal within six weeks or deteriorates.

If you require any further information, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Yours sincerely,

Nurse Sarah Ahmed (Tissue Viability Lead)
